content: /remote-control is active · Continue here, on your phone, or at https://claude.ai/code/session_01U7xmfGWhYKKkuChGE21soU
read https://github.com/u2giants/popcrm-web/blob/main/docs/live-data-implementation-plan.md and tell me what you think.

message.content: read https://github.com/u2giants/popcrm-web/blob/main/docs/live-data-implementation-plan.md and tell me what you think.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Supabase
Here are guidelines for using Supabase tools effectively:

- Before making schema changes, use `list_tables` to understand the existing structure
- When debugging issues, start with `get_logs` and `get_advisors` before making changes
- Use `get_project_url` and `get_publishable_api_key` when helping users configure client-side integrations

If you have access to a local development environment with a filesystem and shell:
- Install the Supabase agent skill for critical development and security guidance: `npx skills add supabase/agent-skills` (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/getting-started/ai-skills.md)
- Use the Supabase CLI (`supabase`) for local development workflows such as starting a local stack, managing migrations, and running edge functions locally (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/local-development.md)
- Prefer local development and testing before applying changes to a remote project

If you are running in a web-only or remote environment without filesystem or shell access:
- Rely on the MCP tools directly for all Supabase interactions
- Use `apply_migration` carefully, as changes go directly to the remote project

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the plan. Since it was just committed to this repo, let me read it locally (faster and authoritative) rather than fetching from GitHub.

input.description: Find the live data plan file

content.content:
1	# Live Data Implementation Plan
2	
3	## Background
4	
5	POP CRM is now backed by Supabase, which should be treated as the live system of
6	record for CRM operations. The current frontend has been migrated to Supabase at
7	the API layer, but the data-loading model still behaves like the older
8	snapshot-style app: `CrmDataContext` fetches many CRM collections during a global
9	bootstrap, stores them in React state, and exposes a manual "Refresh CRM data"
10	action to rerun that bootstrap.
11	
12	That creates the wrong product feeling. Staff experience the CRM as if it is
13	importing or syncing data from another system, even though Supabase is now the
14	backend. The goal is to make the app feel live, immediate, and native to
15	Supabase:
16	
17	- The shell should appear immediately.
18	- Pages should show existing data while quietly refreshing.
19	- User edits should update the UI instantly.
20	- Other users' changes, webhook updates, and automation output should appear
21	  without a manual refresh.
22	- The refresh button should become a fallback, not part of normal workflow.
23	
24	This does not mean the browser holds no state. Every web app keeps a local
25	working set for rendering tables, drawers, filters, counts, and selected records.
26	The change is that local state should be small, page-scoped, cached, and kept in
27	sync with Supabase instead of acting like a manually refreshed copy of the whole
28	CRM.
29	
30	## Current State
31	
32	- Supabase client configuration lives in `src/lib/supabase.ts`.
33	- CRM reads and writes are implemented in `src/features/crm/api.ts`.
34	- Reads go through `api.crm_*` views.
35	- Operational writes go to `crm` tables or guarded `api` RPCs.
36	- `src/features/crm/CrmDataContext.tsx` still performs one global load of many
37	  collections.
38	- `AppHeader` exposes a manual refresh button that calls the global loader.
39	- Many pages depend on globally preloaded arrays from `useCrmData()`.
40	
41	This architecture makes initial navigation heavier than necessary and makes
42	freshness depend on a manual action.
43	
44	## Target Experience
45	
46	The app should feel like this:
47	
48	1. A signed-in user lands in the CRM and sees the app frame immediately.
49	2. The current page loads only the data it needs.
50	3. If cached data exists, it remains visible while a background request checks
51	   for newer data.
52	4. When the user edits a record, the visible row/drawer updates immediately.
53	5. When Supabase reports a relevant table change, the affected page quietly
54	   refetches or patches the changed row.
55	6. Dashboard counts and command search update in the background without blocking
56	   normal work.
57	7. Manual refresh remains available for recovery, but users rarely need it.
58	
59	## Design Principles
60	
61	- Supabase is the source of truth.
62	- The browser keeps only the active working set live.
63	- Page data should be independent; one slow collection must not block the rest
64	  of the app.
65	- Existing data should not disappear during refreshes.
66	- Writes should be optimistic where the failure behavior is easy to explain and
67	  easy to roll back.
68	- Realtime should be applied selectively to high-change surfaces instead of
69	  streaming every table into every browser.
70	- API views may be refetched after realtime events because Supabase Realtime
71	  generally emits changes from base tables, not derived view rows.
72	- The implementation should preserve the existing security model: browser-safe
73	  reads through `api` views, RLS-enforced writes through `crm` tables or guarded
74	  RPCs.
75	
76	## Implementation Phases
77	
78	### Phase 1: Remove the Global Loading Bottleneck
79	
80	Replace the all-CRM bootstrap with page-scoped data loading.
81	
82	- Introduce a small query/cache layer for CRM data.
83	- Prefer TanStack Query unless there is a strong reason to keep a custom cache.
84	- Give each page its own query keys, for example:
85	  - `crm.accounts.curated`
86	  - `crm.accounts.all`
87	  - `crm.contacts.curated`
88	  - `crm.emailRouting.queue`
89	  - `crm.tasks.list`
90	  - `crm.meetings.list`
91	  - `crm.opportunities.list`
92	- Keep previous data visible while refreshing.
93	- Move global stats to their own lightweight queries instead of deriving all
94	  counts from globally loaded arrays.
95	- Keep `CrmDataContext` temporarily as a compatibility wrapper during migration,
96	  then delete it once pages no longer depend on global arrays.
97	
98	Expected result:
99	
100	- The app shell renders immediately.
101	- Navigating to one page does not require loading every CRM collection.
102	- "Loading..." becomes local and brief, not a whole-app state.
103	
104	### Phase 2: Make Writes Feel Instant
105	
106	Update local cached data immediately after successful user actions, and use
107	optimistic updates for simple edits.
108	
109	- For inline table edits, update the row in the page cache immediately.
110	- For drawer edits, update both the selected record and the list row cache.
111	- For create actions, insert the new adapted row into the relevant cache after
112	  Supabase returns the created record.
113	- For drag/status changes, apply an optimistic patch before awaiting the write.
114	- On write failure, revert the optimistic patch and show a clear toast.
115	- Invalidate related queries after writes so views and derived fields reconcile
116	  with Supabase.
117	
118	Expected result:
119	
120	- A user action appears to complete immediately.
121	- Manual refresh is not needed after editing a task, routing an email, or
122	  changing an account/contact field.
123	
124	### Phase 3: Add Background Freshness
125	
126	Make the app quietly refresh at natural moments.
127	
128	- Refetch active page queries when the browser window regains focus.
129	- Refetch active page queries after reconnecting from offline/network loss.
130	- Add conservative background intervals for high-change pages:
131	  - Email Routing: frequent enough to catch new queue items quickly.
132	  - Tasks: moderate interval.
133	  - Meetings and notes: moderate interval.
134	  - Accounts/contacts/opportunities: slower interval or focus-only.
135	- Avoid replacing visible data with loading placeholders during background
136	  refetches.
137	- Add a small non-blocking "updated" or "syncing" affordance only if users need
138	  feedback.
139	
140	Expected result:
141	
142	- Users who leave a tab open see current data when they return.
143	- Webhook- and worker-created records appear without a manual refresh.
144	
145	### Phase 4: Add Supabase Realtime for High-Value Tables
146	
147	Use Supabase Realtime to trigger targeted refetches or patches.
148	
149	Start with tables that change often or where freshness is operationally
150	important:
151	
152	- `crm.email_message`
153	- `crm.task`
154	- `crm.meeting_note`
155	- `crm.note`
156	- `crm.opportunity`
157	- `crm.department`
158	- account/contact source tables in the shared core schema, if exposed to
159	  Realtime and allowed by policy
160	
161	Implementation approach:
162	
163	- Create a small realtime subscription module, for example
164	  `src/features/crm/realtime.ts`.
165	- Subscribe only while the relevant page or app section is mounted.
166	- On insert/update/delete events, invalidate the matching page query.
167	- Debounce bursts of events to avoid refetch storms.
168	- Prefer refetching the relevant `api.crm_*` view for correctness, because the
169	  event payload may not contain joined display fields.
170	- Keep polling/focus refresh as a fallback even after Realtime is added.
171	
172	Expected result:
173	
174	- Other users' changes and automation output show up naturally.
175	- The app feels live without trying to mirror the entire database in memory.
176	
177	### Phase 5: Make Dashboard and Search Lightweight
178	
179	The dashboard and command search should not require loading every operational
180	record.
181	
182	- Add or use lightweight aggregate endpoints/views for dashboard counts.
183	- Keep recent activity panels as bounded queries.
184	- Build command search from dedicated search queries or small cached slices,
185	  not from a global all-record preload.
186	- Load search data lazily when the command palette opens, then cache it.
187	
188	Expected result:
189	
190	- Overview loads quickly.
191	- Search remains fast without forcing global CRM data into memory at startup.
192	
193	### Phase 6: Remove or Reframe Manual Refresh
194	
195	Once page-scoped loading, background refresh, and realtime are working:
196	
197	- Remove "Refresh CRM data" as a primary header action, or move it into a
198	  lower-priority menu as "Sync now".
199	- If retained, make it invalidate active CRM queries rather than rerun a global
200	  bootstrap.
201	- Replace global loading state with per-query pending/background states.
202	
203	Expected result:
204	
205	- Users no longer feel responsible for keeping CRM data current.
206	- Refresh becomes a recovery tool, not normal workflow.
207	
208	## Suggested Technical Shape
209	
210	Use a query client as the center of frontend data freshness:
211	
212	- `QueryClientProvider` at the app root.
213	- CRM query hooks close to the CRM feature, for example:
214	  - `useAccountsQuery()`
215	  - `useContactsQuery()`
216	  - `useEmailRoutingQuery()`
217	  - `useTasksQuery()`
218	  - `useMeetingsQuery()`
219	  - `useOpportunitiesQuery()`
220	- Mutation hooks for writes:
221	  - `useUpdateAccountMutation()`
222	  - `useUpdateContactMutation()`
223	  - `useUpdateTaskMutation()`
224	  - `useUpdateEmailMessageMutation()`
225	- Realtime subscriptions invalidate query keys rather than directly rewriting
226	  every UI shape.
227	
228	Recommended defaults:
229	
230	- `staleTime`: short for operational queues, longer for low-change reference
231	  data.
232	- `gcTime`: long enough to make back/forward navigation instant.
233	- `refetchOnWindowFocus`: enabled.
234	- `placeholderData` / previous data: enabled for list pages.
235	- `retry`: conservative, with visible error states for page-level failures.
236	
237	## Risks and Constraints
238	
239	- Supabase Realtime must be enabled for the relevant tables.
240	- Realtime authorization and RLS behavior must be verified for each schema/table.
241	- Events from base tables may not include joined fields from the `api` views.
242	- High-volume tables can create noisy realtime traffic if subscriptions are too
243	  broad.
244	- Some existing pages assume all CRM arrays are globally available; migration
245	  should be incremental to avoid a large risky rewrite.
246	- Documentation still contains Directus-era language and should be updated as a
247	  separate cleanup so future contributors do not make the wrong architectural
248	  assumptions.
249	
250	## Acceptance Criteria
251	
252	- Initial signed-in shell renders without waiting for all CRM collections.
253	- Opening a page fetches only that page's required data.
254	- Navigating back to a previously visited page shows cached data immediately.
255	- Editing a row or drawer updates the visible UI without clicking refresh.
256	- New email-routing items and task updates appear through background refresh or
257	  realtime.
258	- Manual refresh is no longer needed during normal CRM operation.
259	- No page returns to the old all-or-nothing global loading behavior.
260	- Existing RLS and browser-safe view boundaries remain intact.
261	
262	## Proposed Rollout Order
263	
264	1. Add the query/cache foundation and migrate one low-risk page.
265	2. Migrate Tasks and Email Routing next because users most clearly benefit from
266	   live behavior there.
267	3. Add mutation cache updates for migrated pages.
268	4. Add focus/reconnect refetch globally through the query client.
269	5. Add realtime invalidation for Tasks and Email Routing.
270	6. Migrate remaining pages.
271	7. Replace dashboard/search global dependencies with lightweight queries.
272	8. Remove the global CRM bootstrap and demote the manual refresh action.
273	
274	## Definition of Done
275	
276	The site should feel like a Supabase-native CRM: fast to open, immediate after
277	edits, quietly self-updating, and resilient when one data surface has a problem.
278	Users should trust that what they are seeing is current without needing to press
279	"Refresh CRM data" as part of normal work.
280

file.content:
# Live Data Implementation Plan

## Background

POP CRM is now backed by Supabase, which should be treated as the live system of
record for CRM operations. The current frontend has been migrated to Supabase at
the API layer, but the data-loading model still behaves like the older
snapshot-style app: `CrmDataContext` fetches many CRM collections during a global
bootstrap, stores them in React state, and exposes a manual "Refresh CRM data"
action to rerun that bootstrap.

That creates the wrong product feeling. Staff experience the CRM as if it is
importing or syncing data from another system, even though Supabase is now the
backend. The goal is to make the app feel live, immediate, and native to
Supabase:

- The shell should appear immediately.
- Pages should show existing data while quietly refreshing.
- User edits should update the UI instantly.
- Other users' changes, webhook updates, and automation output should appear
  without a manual refresh.
- The refresh button should become a fallback, not part of normal workflow.

This does not mean the browser holds no state. Every web app keeps a local
working set for rendering tables, drawers, filters, counts, and selected records.
The change is that local state should be small, page-scoped, cached, and kept in
sync with Supabase instead of acting like a manually refreshed copy of the whole
CRM.

## Current State

- Supabase client configuration lives in `src/lib/supabase.ts`.
- CRM reads and writes are implemented in `src/features/crm/api.ts`.
- Reads go through `api.crm_*` views.
- Operational writes go to `crm` tables or guarded `api` RPCs.
- `src/features/crm/CrmDataContext.tsx` still performs one global load of many
  collections.
- `AppHeader` exposes a manual refresh button that calls the global loader.
- Many pages depend on globally preloaded arrays from `useCrmData()`.

This architecture makes initial navigation heavier than necessary and makes
freshness depend on a manual action.

## Target Experience

The app should feel like this:

1. A signed-in user lands in the CRM and sees the app frame immediately.
2. The current page loads only the data it needs.
3. If cached data exists, it remains visible while a background request checks
   for newer data.
4. When the user edits a record, the visible row/drawer updates immediately.
5. When Supabase reports a relevant table change, the affected page quietly
   refetches or patches the changed row.
6. Dashboard counts and command search update in the background without blocking
   normal work.
7. Manual refresh remains available for recovery, but users rarely need it.

## Design Principles

- Supabase is the source of truth.
- The browser keeps only the active working set live.
- Page data should be independent; one slow collection must not block the rest
  of the app.
- Existing data should not disappear during refreshes.
- Writes should be optimistic where the failure behavior is easy to explain and
  easy to roll back.
- Realtime should be applied selectively to high-change surfaces instead of
  streaming every table into every browser.
- API views may be refetched after realtime events because Supabase Realtime
  generally emits changes from base tables, not derived view rows.
- The implementation should preserve the existing security model: browser-safe
  reads through `api` views, RLS-enforced writes through `crm` tables or guarded
  RPCs.

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Remove the Global Loading Bottleneck

Replace the all-CRM bootstrap with page-scoped data loading.

- Introduce a small query/cache layer for CRM data.
- Prefer TanStack Query unless there is a strong reason to keep a custom cache.
- Give each page its own query keys, for example:
  - `crm.accounts.curated`
  - `crm.accounts.all`
  - `crm.contacts.curated`
  - `crm.emailRouting.queue`
  - `crm.tasks.list`
  - `crm.meetings.list`
  - `crm.opportunities.list`
- Keep previous data visible while refreshing.
- Move global stats to their own lightweight queries instead of deriving all
  counts from globally loaded arrays.
- Keep `CrmDataContext` temporarily as a compatibility wrapper during migration,
  then delete it once pages no longer depend on global arrays.

Expected result:

- The app shell renders immediately.
- Navigating to one page does not require loading every CRM collection.
- "Loading..." becomes local and brief, not a whole-app state.

### Phase 2: Make Writes Feel Instant

Update local cached data immediately after successful user actions, and use
optimistic updates for simple edits.

- For inline table edits, update the row in the page cache immediately.
- For drawer edits, update both the selected record and the list row cache.
- For create actions, insert the new adapted row into the relevant cache after
  Supabase returns the created record.
- For drag/status changes, apply an optimistic patch before awaiting the write.
- On write failure, revert the optimistic patch and show a clear toast.
- Invalidate related queries after writes so views and derived fields reconcile
  with Supabase.

Expected result:

- A user action appears to complete immediately.
- Manual refresh is not needed after editing a task, routing an email, or
  changing an account/contact field.

### Phase 3: Add Background Freshness

Make the app quietly refresh at natural moments.

- Refetch active page queries when the browser window regains focus.
- Refetch active page queries after reconnecting from offline/network loss.
- Add conservative background intervals for high-change pages:
  - Email Routing: frequent enough to catch new queue items quickly.
  - Tasks: moderate interval.
  - Meetings and notes: moderate interval.
  - Accounts/contacts/opportunities: slower interval or focus-only.
- Avoid replacing visible data with loading placeholders during background
  refetches.
- Add a small non-blocking "updated" or "syncing" affordance only if users need
  feedback.

Expected result:

- Users who leave a tab open see current data when they return.
- Webhook- and worker-created records appear without a manual refresh.

### Phase 4: Add Supabase Realtime for High-Value Tables

Use Supabase Realtime to trigger targeted refetches or patches.

Start with tables that change often or where freshness is operationally
important:

- `crm.email_message`
- `crm.task`
- `crm.meeting_note`
- `crm.note`
- `crm.opportunity`
- `crm.department`
- account/contact source tables in the shared core schema, if exposed to
  Realtime and allowed by policy

Implementation approach:

- Create a small realtime subscription module, for example
  `src/features/crm/realtime.ts`.
- Subscribe only while the relevant page or app section is mounted.
- On insert/update/delete events, invalidate the matching page query.
- Debounce bursts of events to avoid refetch storms.
- Prefer refetching the relevant `api.crm_*` view for correctness, because the
  event payload may not contain joined display fields.
- Keep polling/focus refresh as a fallback even after Realtime is added.

Expected result:

- Other users' changes and automation output show up naturally.
- The app feels live without trying to mirror the entire database in memory.

### Phase 5: Make Dashboard and Search Lightweight

The dashboard and command search should not require loading every operational
record.

- Add or use lightweight aggregate endpoints/views for dashboard counts.
- Keep recent activity panels as bounded queries.
- Build command search from dedicated search queries or small cached slices,
  not from a global all-record preload.
- Load search data lazily when the command palette opens, then cache it.

Expected result:

- Overview loads quickly.
- Search remains fast without forcing global CRM data into memory at startup.

### Phase 6: Remove or Reframe Manual Refresh

Once page-scoped loading, background refresh, and realtime are working:

- Remove "Refresh CRM data" as a primary header action, or move it into a
  lower-priority menu as "Sync now".
- If retained, make it invalidate active CRM queries rather than rerun a global
  bootstrap.
- Replace global loading state with per-query pending/background states.

Expected result:

- Users no longer feel responsible for keeping CRM data current.
- Refresh becomes a recovery tool, not normal workflow.

## Suggested Technical Shape

Use a query client as the center of frontend data freshness:

- `QueryClientProvider` at the app root.
- CRM query hooks close to the CRM feature, for example:
  - `useAccountsQuery()`
  - `useContactsQuery()`
  - `useEmailRoutingQuery()`
  - `useTasksQuery()`
  - `useMeetingsQuery()`
  - `useOpportunitiesQuery()`
- Mutation hooks for writes:
  - `useUpdateAccountMutation()`
  - `useUpdateContactMutation()`
  - `useUpdateTaskMutation()`
  - `useUpdateEmailMessageMutation()`
- Realtime subscriptions invalidate query keys rather than directly rewriting
  every UI shape.

Recommended defaults:

- `staleTime`: short for operational queues, longer for low-change reference
  data.
- `gcTime`: long enough to make back/forward navigation instant.
- `refetchOnWindowFocus`: enabled.
- `placeholderData` / previous data: enabled for list pages.
- `retry`: conservative, with visible error states for page-level failures.

## Risks and Constraints

- Supabase Realtime must be enabled for the relevant tables.
- Realtime authorization and RLS behavior must be verified for each schema/table.
- Events from base tables may not include joined fields from the `api` views.
- High-volume tables can create noisy realtime traffic if subscriptions are too
  broad.
- Some existing pages assume all CRM arrays are globally available; migration
  should be incremental to avoid a large risky rewrite.
- Documentation still contains Directus-era language and should be updated as a
  separate cleanup so future contributors do not make the wrong architectural
  assumptions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Initial signed-in shell renders without waiting for all CRM collections.
- Opening a page fetches only that page's required data.
- Navigating back to a previously visited page shows cached data immediately.
- Editing a row or drawer updates the visible UI without clicking refresh.
- New email-routing items and task updates appear through background refresh or
  realtime.
- Manual refresh is no longer needed during normal CRM operation.
- No page returns to the old all-or-nothing global loading behavior.
- Existing RLS and browser-safe view boundaries remain intact.

## Proposed Rollout Order

1. Add the query/cache foundation and migrate one low-risk page.
2. Migrate Tasks and Email Routing next because users most clearly benefit from
   live behavior there.
3. Add mutation cache updates for migrated pages.
4. Add focus/reconnect refetch globally through the query client.
5. Add realtime invalidation for Tasks and Email Routing.
6. Migrate remaining pages.
7. Replace dashboard/search global dependencies with lightweight queries.
8. Remove the global CRM bootstrap and demote the manual refresh action.

## Definition of Done

The site should feel like a Supabase-native CRM: fast to open, immediate after
edits, quietly self-updating, and resilient when one data surface has a problem.
Users should trust that what they are seeing is current without needing to press
"Refresh CRM data" as part of normal work.

lastPrompt: read https://github.com/u2giants/popcrm-web/blob/main/docs/live-data-implementation-plan.md and tell me what you think.

content.text: Let me ground my opinion in the actual code the plan describes.

1	import {
2	  createContext,
3	  useCallback,
4	  useContext,
5	  useEffect,
6	  useMemo,
7	  useState,
8	  type Dispatch,
9	  type ReactNode,
10	  type SetStateAction,
11	} from 'react'
12	import {
13	  fetchAiModelConfigs,
14	  fetchApprovalThreads,
15	  fetchBuyers,
16	  fetchDepartments,
17	  fetchEmailMessages,
18	  fetchIgnoreRules,
19	  fetchIngestedContacts,
20	  fetchIngestedDomains,
21	  fetchMeetingNotes,
22	  fetchNotes,
23	  fetchOpportunities,
24	  fetchRetailers,
25	  fetchTasks,
26	} from './api'
27	import { FIREFLIES_HEALTH_URL, isApprovalResolved, needsRouting } from './constants'
28	import type {
29	  Buyer,
30	  CrmAiModelConfig,
31	  CrmDepartment,
32	  CrmEmailMessage,
33	  CrmIgnoreRule,
34	  CrmLicensorApprovalThread,
35	  CrmMeetingNote,
36	  CrmNote,
37	  CrmOpportunity,
38	  CrmTask,
39	  Retailer,
40	} from '@/lib/types'
41	
42	export interface CrmDashboardStats {
43	  accounts: number
44	  contacts: number
45	  openOpportunities: number
46	  emails: number
47	  needsRouting: number
48	  routed: number
49	  skipped: number
50	  companyOnly: number
51	  companyDept: number
52	  meetings: number
53	  openTasks: number
54	  pendingApprovals: number
55	}
56	
57	interface CrmDataValue {
58	  loading: boolean
59	  error: boolean
60	  refresh: () => void
61	  firefliesOk: boolean | null
62	
63	  opportunities: CrmOpportunity[]
64	  retailers: Retailer[]
65	  buyers: Buyer[]
66	  // Full ingested registries — used only by the triage pages (Accounts, Contacts,
67	  // Email Routing, Meetings). Operational pages/pickers use retailers/buyers (customers).
68	  ingestedDomains: Retailer[]
69	  ingestedContacts: Buyer[]
70	  departments: CrmDepartment[]
71	  emails: CrmEmailMessage[]
72	  meetings: CrmMeetingNote[]
73	  ignoreRules: CrmIgnoreRule[]
74	  aiConfigs: CrmAiModelConfig[]
75	  notes: CrmNote[]
76	  tasks: CrmTask[]
77	  approvals: CrmLicensorApprovalThread[]
78	
79	  setOpportunities: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmOpportunity[]>>
80	  setRetailers: Dispatch<SetStateAction<Retailer[]>>
81	  setBuyers: Dispatch<SetStateAction<Buyer[]>>
82	  setIngestedDomains: Dispatch<SetStateAction<Retailer[]>>
83	  setIngestedContacts: Dispatch<SetStateAction<Buyer[]>>
84	  setDepartments: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmDepartment[]>>
85	  setEmails: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmEmailMessage[]>>
86	  setMeetings: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmMeetingNote[]>>
87	  setIgnoreRules: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmIgnoreRule[]>>
88	  setAiConfigs: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmAiModelConfig[]>>
89	  setNotes: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmNote[]>>
90	  setTasks: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmTask[]>>
91	  setApprovals: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmLicensorApprovalThread[]>>
92	
93	  stats: CrmDashboardStats
94	}
95	
96	const CrmDataContext = createContext<CrmDataValue | null>(null)
97	
98	const OPEN_TASK = (t: CrmTask) => t.status !== 'DONE' && t.status !== 'CANCELED'
99	const PENDING_APPROVAL = (a: CrmLicensorApprovalThread) => !isApprovalResolved(a.stage)
100	
101	export function CrmDataProvider({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
102	  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true)
103	  const [error, setError] = useState(false)
104	  const [firefliesOk, setFirefliesOk] = useState<boolean | null>(null)
105	
106	  const [opportunities, setOpportunities] = useState<CrmOpportunity[]>([])
107	  const [retailers, setRetailers] = useState<Retailer[]>([])
108	  const [buyers, setBuyers] = useState<Buyer[]>([])
109	  const [ingestedDomains, setIngestedDomains] = useState<Retailer[]>([])
110	  const [ingestedContacts, setIngestedContacts] = useState<Buyer[]>([])
111	  const [departments, setDepartments] = useState<CrmDepartment[]>([])
112	  const [emails, setEmails] = useState<CrmEmailMessage[]>([])
113	  const [meetings, setMeetings] = useState<CrmMeetingNote[]>([])
114	  const [ignoreRules, setIgnoreRules] = useState<CrmIgnoreRule[]>([])
115	  const [aiConfigs, setAiConfigs] = useState<CrmAiModelConfig[]>([])
116	  const [notes, setNotes] = useState<CrmNote[]>([])
117	  const [tasks, setTasks] = useState<CrmTask[]>([])
118	  const [approvals, setApprovals] = useState<CrmLicensorApprovalThread[]>([])
119	
120	  const load = useCallback(async () => {
121	    setLoading(true)
122	    setError(false)
123	
124	    // Load every collection independently. One collection being forbidden
125	    // (e.g. a Directus permission gap) or failing must NOT blank the whole app —
126	    // that section just stays empty. We only surface a hard error if everything
127	    // fails (auth/network down).
128	    const sources: Array<{ name: string; run: () => Promise<unknown>; set: (v: never) => void }> = [
129	      { name: 'opportunities', run: fetchOpportunities, set: setOpportunities as (v: never) => void },
130	      { name: 'accounts', run: () => fetchRetailers(-1), set: setRetailers as (v: never) => void },
131	      { name: 'contacts', run: () => fetchBuyers(-1), set: setBuyers as (v: never) => void },
132	      { name: 'ingested domains', run: () => fetchIngestedDomains(-1), set: setIngestedDomains as (v: never) => void },
133	      { name: 'ingested contacts', run: () => fetchIngestedContacts(-1), set: setIngestedContacts as (v: never) => void },
134	      { name: 'departments', run: () => fetchDepartments(), set: setDepartments as (v: never) => void },
135	      { name: 'emails', run: () => fetchEmailMessages(-1), set: setEmails as (v: never) => void },
136	      { name: 'meetings', run: () => fetchMeetingNotes(-1), set: setMeetings as (v: never) => void },
137	      { name: 'ignore rules', run: fetchIgnoreRules, set: setIgnoreRules as (v: never) => void },
138	      { name: 'AI config', run: fetchAiModelConfigs, set: setAiConfigs as (v: never) => void },
139	      { name: 'notes', run: fetchNotes, set: setNotes as (v: never) => void },
140	      { name: 'tasks', run: fetchTasks, set: setTasks as (v: never) => void },
141	      { name: 'approvals', run: fetchApprovalThreads, set: setApprovals as (v: never) => void },
142	    ]
143	
144	    const results = await Promise.allSettled(sources.map((s) => s.run()))
145	    const failed: string[] = []
146	    results.forEach((result, i) => {
147	      if (result.status === 'fulfilled') {
148	        sources[i].set(result.value as never)
149	      } else {
150	        failed.push(sources[i].name)
151	        sources[i].set([] as never)
152	      }
153	    })
154	
155	    if (failed.length) {
156	      console.warn(`CRM: could not load ${failed.join(', ')} (continuing with the rest)`)
157	    }
158	    // Hard error only when nothing loaded at all.
159	    setError(failed.length === sources.length)
160	    setLoading(false)
161	  }, [])
162	
163	  useEffect(() => {
164	    queueMicrotask(() => void load())
165	    queueMicrotask(() => {
166	      fetch(FIREFLIES_HEALTH_URL)
167	        .then((res) => setFirefliesOk(res.ok))
168	        .catch(() => setFirefliesOk(false))
169	    })
170	  }, [load])
171	
172	  const stats = useMemo<CrmDashboardStats>(() => {
173	    const by = (status: string) => emails.filter((e) => e.routing_status === status).length
174	    return {
175	      accounts: retailers.length,
176	      contacts: buyers.length,
177	      openOpportunities: opportunities.filter((o) => o.stage !== 'CLOSED').length,
178	      emails: emails.length,
179	      needsRouting: emails.filter((e) => needsRouting(e.routing_status)).length,
180	      routed: by('ROUTED'),
181	      skipped: by('SKIPPED'),
182	      companyOnly: by('COMPANY_ONLY'),
183	      companyDept: by('COMPANY_DEPT'),
184	      meetings: meetings.length,
185	      openTasks: tasks.filter(OPEN_TASK).length,
186	      pendingApprovals: approvals.filter(PENDING_APPROVAL).length,
187	    }
188	  }, [retailers, buyers, opportunities, emails, meetings, tasks, approvals])
189	
190	  const value: CrmDataValue = {
191	    loading,
192	    error,
193	    refresh: () => void load(),
194	    firefliesOk,
195	    opportunities,
196	    retailers,
197	    buyers,
198	    ingestedDomains,
199	    ingestedContacts,
200	    departments,
201	    emails,
202	    meetings,
203	    ignoreRules,
204	    aiConfigs,
205	    notes,
206	    tasks,
207	    approvals,
208	    setOpportunities,
209	    setRetailers,
210	    setBuyers,
211	    setIngestedDomains,
212	    setIngestedContacts,
213	    setDepartments,
214	    setEmails,
215	    setMeetings,
216	    setIgnoreRules,
217	    setAiConfigs,
218	    setNotes,
219	    setTasks,
220	    setApprovals,
221	    stats,
222	  }
223	
224	  return <CrmDataContext.Provider value={value}>{children}</CrmDataContext.Provider>
225	}
226	
227	export function useCrmData() {
228	  const ctx = useContext(CrmDataContext)
229	  if (!ctx) throw new Error('useCrmData must be used within CrmDataProvider')
230	  return ctx
231	}

file.content:
import {
  createContext,
  useCallback,
  useContext,
  useEffect,
  useMemo,
  useState,
  type Dispatch,
  type ReactNode,
  type SetStateAction,
} from 'react'
import {
  fetchAiModelConfigs,
  fetchApprovalThreads,
  fetchBuyers,
  fetchDepartments,
  fetchEmailMessages,
  fetchIgnoreRules,
  fetchIngestedContacts,
  fetchIngestedDomains,
  fetchMeetingNotes,
  fetchNotes,
  fetchOpportunities,
  fetchRetailers,
  fetchTasks,
} from './api'
import { FIREFLIES_HEALTH_URL, isApprovalResolved, needsRouting } from './constants'
import type {
  Buyer,
  CrmAiModelConfig,
  CrmDepartment,
  CrmEmailMessage,
  CrmIgnoreRule,
  CrmLicensorApprovalThread,
  CrmMeetingNote,
  CrmNote,
  CrmOpportunity,
  CrmTask,
  Retailer,
} from '@/lib/types'

export interface CrmDashboardStats {
  accounts: number
  contacts: number
  openOpportunities: number
  emails: number
  needsRouting: number
  routed: number
  skipped: number
  companyOnly: number
  companyDept: number
  meetings: number
  openTasks: number
  pendingApprovals: number
}

interface CrmDataValue {
  loading: boolean
  error: boolean
  refresh: () => void
  firefliesOk: boolean | null

  opportunities: CrmOpportunity[]
  retailers: Retailer[]
  buyers: Buyer[]
  // Full ingested registries — used only by the triage pages (Accounts, Contacts,
  // Email Routing, Meetings). Operational pages/pickers use retailers/buyers (customers).
  ingestedDomains: Retailer[]
  ingestedContacts: Buyer[]
  departments: CrmDepartment[]
  emails: CrmEmailMessage[]
  meetings: CrmMeetingNote[]
  ignoreRules: CrmIgnoreRule[]
  aiConfigs: CrmAiModelConfig[]
  notes: CrmNote[]
  tasks: CrmTask[]
  approvals: CrmLicensorApprovalThread[]

  setOpportunities: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmOpportunity[]>>
  setRetailers: Dispatch<SetStateAction<Retailer[]>>
  setBuyers: Dispatch<SetStateAction<Buyer[]>>
  setIngestedDomains: Dispatch<SetStateAction<Retailer[]>>
  setIngestedContacts: Dispatch<SetStateAction<Buyer[]>>
  setDepartments: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmDepartment[]>>
  setEmails: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmEmailMessage[]>>
  setMeetings: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmMeetingNote[]>>
  setIgnoreRules: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmIgnoreRule[]>>
  setAiConfigs: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmAiModelConfig[]>>
  setNotes: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmNote[]>>
  setTasks: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmTask[]>>
  setApprovals: Dispatch<SetStateAction<CrmLicensorApprovalThread[]>>

  stats: CrmDashboardStats
}

const CrmDataContext = createContext<CrmDataValue | null>(null)

const OPEN_TASK = (t: CrmTask) => t.status !== 'DONE' && t.status !== 'CANCELED'
const PENDING_APPROVAL = (a: CrmLicensorApprovalThread) => !isApprovalResolved(a.stage)

export function CrmDataProvider({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true)
  const [error, setError] = useState(false)
  const [firefliesOk, setFirefliesOk] = useState<boolean | null>(null)

  const [opportunities, setOpportunities] = useState<CrmOpportunity[]>([])
  const [retailers, setRetailers] = useState<Retailer[]>([])
  const [buyers, setBuyers] = useState<Buyer[]>([])
  const [ingestedDomains, setIngestedDomains] = useState<Retailer[]>([])
  const [ingestedContacts, setIngestedContacts] = useState<Buyer[]>([])
  const [departments, setDepartments] = useState<CrmDepartment[]>([])
  const [emails, setEmails] = useState<CrmEmailMessage[]>([])
  const [meetings, setMeetings] = useState<CrmMeetingNote[]>([])
  const [ignoreRules, setIgnoreRules] = useState<CrmIgnoreRule[]>([])
  const [aiConfigs, setAiConfigs] = useState<CrmAiModelConfig[]>([])
  const [notes, setNotes] = useState<CrmNote[]>([])
  const [tasks, setTasks] = useState<CrmTask[]>([])
  const [approvals, setApprovals] = useState<CrmLicensorApprovalThread[]>([])

  const load = useCallback(async () => {
    setLoading(true)
    setError(false)

    // Load every collection independently. One collection being forbidden
    // (e.g. a Directus permission gap) or failing must NOT blank the whole app —
    // that section just stays empty. We only surface a hard error if everything
    // fails (auth/network down).
    const sources: Array<{ name: string; run: () => Promise<unknown>; set: (v: never) => void }> = [
      { name: 'opportunities', run: fetchOpportunities, set: setOpportunities as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'accounts', run: () => fetchRetailers(-1), set: setRetailers as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'contacts', run: () => fetchBuyers(-1), set: setBuyers as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'ingested domains', run: () => fetchIngestedDomains(-1), set: setIngestedDomains as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'ingested contacts', run: () => fetchIngestedContacts(-1), set: setIngestedContacts as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'departments', run: () => fetchDepartments(), set: setDepartments as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'emails', run: () => fetchEmailMessages(-1), set: setEmails as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'meetings', run: () => fetchMeetingNotes(-1), set: setMeetings as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'ignore rules', run: fetchIgnoreRules, set: setIgnoreRules as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'AI config', run: fetchAiModelConfigs, set: setAiConfigs as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'notes', run: fetchNotes, set: setNotes as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'tasks', run: fetchTasks, set: setTasks as (v: never) => void },
      { name: 'approvals', run: fetchApprovalThreads, set: setApprovals as (v: never) => void },
    ]

    const results = await Promise.allSettled(sources.map((s) => s.run()))
    const failed: string[] = []
    results.forEach((result, i) => {
      if (result.status === 'fulfilled') {
        sources[i].set(result.value as never)
      } else {
        failed.push(sources[i].name)
        sources[i].set([] as never)
      }
    })

    if (failed.length) {
      console.warn(`CRM: could not load ${failed.join(', ')} (continuing with the rest)`)
    }
    // Hard error only when nothing loaded at all.
    setError(failed.length === sources.length)
    setLoading(false)
  }, [])

  useEffect(() => {
    queueMicrotask(() => void load())
    queueMicrotask(() => {
      fetch(FIREFLIES_HEALTH_URL)
        .then((res) => setFirefliesOk(res.ok))
        .catch(() => setFirefliesOk(false))
    })
  }, [load])

  const stats = useMemo<CrmDashboardStats>(() => {
    const by = (status: string) => emails.filter((e) => e.routing_status === status).length
    return {
      accounts: retailers.length,
      contacts: buyers.length,
      openOpportunities: opportunities.filter((o) => o.stage !== 'CLOSED').length,
      emails: emails.length,
      needsRouting: emails.filter((e) => needsRouting(e.routing_status)).length,
      routed: by('ROUTED'),
      skipped: by('SKIPPED'),
      companyOnly: by('COMPANY_ONLY'),
      companyDept: by('COMPANY_DEPT'),
      meetings: meetings.length,
      openTasks: tasks.filter(OPEN_TASK).length,
      pendingApprovals: approvals.filter(PENDING_APPROVAL).length,
    }
  }, [retailers, buyers, opportunities, emails, meetings, tasks, approvals])

  const value: CrmDataValue = {
    loading,
    error,
    refresh: () => void load(),
    firefliesOk,
    opportunities,
    retailers,
    buyers,
    ingestedDomains,
    ingestedContacts,
    departments,
    emails,
    meetings,
    ignoreRules,
    aiConfigs,
    notes,
    tasks,
    approvals,
    setOpportunities,
    setRetailers,
    setBuyers,
    setIngestedDomains,
    setIngestedContacts,
    setDepartments,
    setEmails,
    setMeetings,
    setIgnoreRules,
    setAiConfigs,
    setNotes,
    setTasks,
    setApprovals,
    stats,
  }

  return <CrmDataContext.Provider value={value}>{children}</CrmDataContext.Provider>
}

export function useCrmData() {
  const ctx = useContext(CrmDataContext)
  if (!ctx) throw new Error('useCrmData must be used within CrmDataProvider')
  return ctx
}

content.text:
Read it, and cross-checked it against the actual code. Here's my honest take.

## Verdict: the plan is solid and correctly diagnosed. TanStack Query is the right call.

The diagnosis matches reality. `CrmDataContext.tsx` does exactly what the plan says — one `Promise.allSettled` over **13 collections** on mount (`CrmDataContext.tsx:128-144`), most of them called with `(-1)` meaning *fetch every row* (`fetchRetailers(-1)`, `fetchEmailMessages(-1)`, etc.). And **26 files** consume `useCrmData()`. So the "snapshot app" framing is accurate, not strawman. TanStack Query isn't installed yet, so this is a genuine foundation change, not a tweak.

The phasing (foundation → optimistic writes → focus/reconnect → realtime → lightweight dashboard → demote refresh) is the right order, and "migrate one low-risk page first, keep `CrmDataContext` as a compatibility wrapper" is the correct incremental strategy. I'd green-light the direction.

## Four things the plan underweights

**1. The real migration cost is the setters, not the query hooks.** The context doesn't just expose arrays — it exposes `setTasks`, `setEmails`, `setOpportunities`, … `SetStateAction` dispatchers (`CrmDataContext.tsx:79-91`). Pages today do their *own* optimistic updates by calling those setters directly. That's almost certainly where most of the 26 call sites' logic lives. Phase 2 ("make writes feel instant") is written as if optimistic updates are new, but they already exist — scattered and hand-rolled. The work is *relocating* that logic into mutation hooks, and a query-cache-backed compatibility wrapper **can't cleanly re-expose a `setTasks(prev => …)` setter**. So the "temporary compatibility wrapper" will be leakier than the plan implies. I'd call this out as the main risk, ahead of realtime.

**2. Page-scoped ≠ bounded.** Splitting the bootstrap removes the *startup* cost, but if `useTasksQuery()` still calls `fetchTasks()` with no limit, you've just moved an unbounded fetch from boot to page-open. The plan should explicitly add server-side pagination/filtering as part of Phase 1, not treat "page-scoped" as sufficient on its own.

**3. Realtime + dashboard aggregates are shared-db work, and the plan doesn't flag the coordination gate.** Enabling Realtime on `crm.*` tables means touching the publication and the RLS/authorization for the realtime role — and Phase 5's "lightweight aggregate endpoints/views" means new `api` views or RPCs. Per this repo's `CLAUDE.md`, anything touching the shared Supabase schema goes through `shared-db/AGENTS.md` (branch + PR), not `main`-only app commits. The plan lists "Realtime must be enabled" as a generic risk but should tie Phases 4 and 5 to that cross-app process explicitly, because it changes *how* those phases ship.

**4. The plan is right that you refetch the view, not patch from the event.** Reads go through `api.crm_*` views but Realtime emits base-table rows without the joined display fields — the plan calls this out (lines 71, 169-170) and that's the correct, non-obvious instinct. Good. Just don't let "debounce bursts" become an afterthought; on `crm.email_message` it's the difference between live and a refetch storm.

## Minor

- The `firefliesOk` health check rides along in this same context (`CrmDataContext.tsx:104,165-169`) — it needs a new home when the context is dismantled; easy to overlook.
- "Documentation still has Directus-era language" (line 246) — agreed it's a separate cleanup, but the comment at `CrmDataContext.tsx:125` literally still says "Directus permission gap." That's a tell that the mental model in the code hasn't fully caught up to Supabase yet, which reinforces why this plan matters.

Net: I'd approve it. My one substantive edit would be to **promote the setter/compatibility-wrapper coupling to the top risk** and **fold bounded/paginated fetching into Phase 1**, since those are where an incremental migration actually gets hard. Want me to draft those revisions into the doc?

content: You asked for my opinion on the live-data implementation plan. I reviewed it against the code and gave a verdict: solid plan, approve it, with two suggested edits. Next action is your call on whether I draft those revisions into the doc. (disable recaps in /config)
you said "Four things the plan underweights", then you said " My one substantive edit". So are there 4 problems/shortcomings to this plan? or 1?

message.content: you said "Four things the plan underweights", then you said " My one substantive edit". So are there 4 problems/shortcomings to this plan? or 1?

content.text:
Fair catch — that's inconsistent. Let me reconcile it honestly.

There are **two** real shortcomings. The other two items weren't problems with the plan.

Sorting my four points by what they actually are:

1. **The setter coupling** — a genuine shortcoming. The plan misjudges where the work and risk are.
2. **Page-scoped ≠ bounded** — a genuine shortcoming. A real gap (unbounded fetches survive the migration) that the plan doesn't address.
3. **Shared-db coordination gate** — *not* a flaw in the plan's thinking, just a process hook this repo requires. It's a "don't forget the branch+PR rule," not "the plan is wrong."
4. **Refetch-the-view instinct** — that was me *agreeing* with the plan. It's praise, not a shortcoming. It shouldn't have been under a "underweights" heading at all — my mistake for filing it there.

So: **2 shortcomings** (#1 and #2), 1 process reminder (#3), 1 point of praise (#4).

Then "my one substantive edit" was sloppy wording. I meant the single highest-priority change — promoting the setter coupling to top risk. But folding bounded fetching into Phase 1 is a second substantive edit. So it should have read "my two substantive edits," matching the two shortcomings.

Bottom line: **2 problems worth fixing in the doc**, not 4 and not 1.

message.content: give me a detailed prompt to give the developer who created this plan as to what you'd improve and why

content.text:
Here's a prompt you can hand directly to the plan's author. I've grounded every point in the actual code so it's defensible, not vibes.

---

> **Re: Live Data Implementation Plan — requested revisions before we start building**
>
> The plan is well-reasoned and I want to build it — the diagnosis is accurate (I confirmed `CrmDataContext` eagerly loads 13 collections on mount via `Promise.allSettled`, most with unbounded `(-1)` fetches, and 26 files consume `useCrmData()`), the phasing order is sensible, and TanStack Query is the right foundation. Two substantive gaps I want addressed in the doc before Phase 1, plus one process hook and one thing I want preserved.
>
> **1. Re-scope Phase 2 around the existing setters — this is the top migration risk, not realtime.**
> The plan treats optimistic writes as something we're *adding* in Phase 2. We're not — they already exist, hand-rolled and scattered. `CrmDataContext` exposes raw `SetStateAction` dispatchers (`setTasks`, `setEmails`, `setOpportunities`, … lines 79–91), and the 26 consumer files almost certainly mutate cached data by calling those setters directly today. The actual work is *relocating* that logic into mutation hooks, not inventing it.
>
> This also breaks the "keep `CrmDataContext` as a compatibility wrapper" strategy (Phase 1) more than the plan admits: a query-cache-backed wrapper **cannot cleanly re-expose a `setTasks(prev => …)` functional setter**. Please:
> - Audit the 26 call sites and categorize each as read-only vs. setter-using before we migrate page one.
> - Decide explicitly how the compatibility wrapper handles setter call sites — my guess is they can't be wrapped and must convert to mutations in the same PR that migrates their page.
> - Move "setter coupling" into the **Risks** section as the #1 risk, ahead of realtime/RLS.
>
> **2. Fold bounded/paginated fetching into Phase 1 — "page-scoped" is not the same as "bounded."**
> Splitting the global bootstrap removes the *startup* cost, but if `useTasksQuery()` still calls `fetchTasks()` with no limit, we've just moved an unbounded full-table fetch from boot to page-open. That doesn't deliver the "fast, live" feeling for anyone with real data volume. Please add server-side pagination / filtering (and decide page sizes per surface) as an explicit Phase 1 deliverable, not an afterthought. The acceptance criterion "opening a page fetches only that page's required data" should mean *a bounded slice*, not *all rows for that one collection*.
>
> **3. Tie Phases 4 and 5 to the shared-db coordination process.**
> Enabling Realtime on `crm.*` tables (Phase 4) means touching the Supabase publication and the realtime-role RLS/authorization; the dashboard "lightweight aggregate endpoints/views" (Phase 5) means new `api` views or RPCs. Per this repo's `CLAUDE.md`, anything touching the shared schema goes through `shared-db/AGENTS.md` (branch + PR), **not** our `main`-only app flow. The plan lists "Realtime must be enabled" as a generic risk — please make it explicit that Phases 4 and 5 each have a shared-db PR as a prerequisite, so we don't plan them as pure frontend work.
>
> **4. Keep the "refetch the view, don't patch from the event" instinct — it's correct.**
> Lines 71 and 169–170 already say it: reads go through `api.crm_*` views, but Realtime emits base-table rows without the joined display fields, so we refetch the view rather than splicing the event payload. That's the right, non-obvious call — keep it. One add: specify the debounce window per high-volume table (`crm.email_message` especially), because the gap between "live" and "refetch storm" is entirely in that number.
>
> **Two smaller things to resolve while you're in there:**
> - The `firefliesOk` health check currently lives inside `CrmDataContext` (lines 104, 165–169). When the context is dismantled it needs a new home — name where it goes.
> - The comment at `CrmDataContext.tsx:125` still says "Directus permission gap." That Directus-era language is a sign the code's mental model hasn't fully moved to Supabase — worth sweeping as part of the cleanup the plan already mentions (line 246).
>
> Net: I'm not asking to re-architect the plan — phases and order stay. I want (1) and (2) reflected before we cut the first PR, and (3) acknowledged so the schema work isn't a surprise mid-phase.

---

Want me to also turn this into a set of inline diffs against the actual `docs/live-data-implementation-plan.md` so the author gets concrete edits instead of prose?
